content.content:
Client:
 Version:    28.3.3
 Context:    desktop-linux

toolUseResult.stdout:
Client:
 Version:    28.3.3
 Context:    desktop-linux

content.subject: Task 1: Update Plugin.shutdown doc-comment
content.description: Replace doc-comment on Plugin.shutdown? slot in packages/core/src/plugin.ts:22-36 to reflect kernel-driven lifecycle. No behavioral change. Run pnpm test --filter @ax/core to confirm green. Commit as docs(core).

content.subject: Task 2: Add KernelHandle + bootstrap shutdown
content.description: Central kernel-side change. Add KernelHandle type, change bootstrap return type to Promise<KernelHandle>, add shutdownTimeoutMs/onShutdownError options, implement reverse-topological shutdown loop, add init-failure rollback, idempotent handle. TDD: write 7 failing tests first in bootstrap.test.ts. Commit as feat(core).

content.subject: Task 3: database-postgres shutdown
content.description: Add async shutdown() to createDatabasePostgresPlugin that calls kysely.destroy(). Drop TODO(kernel-shutdown) at packages/database-postgres/src/plugin.ts:60. Commit.

content.subject: Task 4: storage-sqlite shutdown
content.description: Add async shutdown() to createStorageSqlitePlugin that calls db.destroy(). Drop TODO(kernel-shutdown) at packages/storage-sqlite/src/plugin.ts:22. Commit.

content.subject: Task 5: Drop EventbusPostgresPlugin interface
content.description: Delete EventbusPostgresPlugin interface, return plain Plugin from factory. Drop re-export from index.ts. Update tests to drive shutdown via createTestHarness().close() instead of casting to escape-hatch interface. Commit.

content.subject: Task 6: Drop SessionPostgresPlugin interface
content.description: Delete SessionPostgresPlugin interface, return plain Plugin from factory. Drop re-export. Update doc-comment on hand-rolled init-failure rollback (try/catch stays — kernel does NOT call shutdown on the failing plugin). Update tests to use harness.close(). Commit.

content.subject: Task 7: Verify sandbox-k8s cleanup needs
content.description: Verify whether @kubernetes/client-node@1.x retains a per-client http.Agent that needs explicit destroy(). Expected: no — uses node-fetch with global agent. Document finding in plugin.ts. If a per-client agent IS retained, add shutdown(). Commit.

content.subject: Task 8: Drop IpcHttpPlugin interface
content.description: Delete IpcHttpPlugin interface, fold closeListener() body into Plugin.shutdown slot. Return plain Plugin. Audit/update any closeListener call sites (serve command will be updated in Task 11). Commit.

content.subject: Task 9: Verify eventbus-inprocess + workspace-git
content.description: Audit eventbus-inprocess, workspace-git, workspace-git-http (host-side) for long-lived handles/timers/agents. Expected: nothing to clean up. Optional doc comment. Commit only if changes were needed.

content.subject: Task 10: Wire CLI main + credentials + mcp
content.description: Capture KernelHandle from bootstrap() in packages/cli/src/main.ts:225 and packages/cli/src/commands/{credentials,mcp}.ts. Wrap chat:run / commands in try/finally calling handle.shutdown() in the finally. Document SIGINT-mid-chat as out of scope. Commit.

content.subject: Task 11: Wire serve SIGINT/SIGTERM to handle.shutdown
content.description: Update packages/cli/src/commands/serve.ts: capture handle, refactor signal handler to (1) close HTTP listener, (2) call handle.shutdown(), (3) exit 0. Second-signal force-exits 130/143. Handler must be installed before bootstrap (capture handle into a closure variable readable at signal time, not handler install time). Commit.

content.subject: Task 12: Final sweep + verification
content.description: Grep for TODO(kernel-shutdown), EventbusPostgresPlugin, SessionPostgresPlugin, IpcHttpPlugin — expect zero matches in src/. Run pnpm build and pnpm test across the monorepo, expect all green.

content.content:
100	The honest list of what this slice doesn't do, and why.
101	
102	### Plain HTTP within the cluster, no mTLS
103	
104	We use plain HTTP between runner pods and the host. NetworkPolicy is the perimeter; bearer auth is the wall behind it. mTLS would be stronger, and we'll add it when a real adversary makes the math change.
105	
106	This is a documented choice, not an oversight. NetworkPolicy + bearer auth is the same posture legacy v1 used in production, and on a properly-policied cluster it confines reach to the runner pods we just spawned. mTLS adds CA bootstrapping, cert rotation, and a chunk of operational surface; we'd rather get the transport landed and harden later than ship something we can't operate.
107	
108	The threat we're explicit about: if NetworkPolicy is disabled or unsupported (some kind clusters' default CNI doesn't enforce policies), an attacker reachable on the cluster network could attempt requests against the listener. Bearer auth still blocks the call without a stolen token, and tokens don't leak from the host (they're held in the session store and minted per session). But "NetworkPolicy is the perimeter" is a real prerequisite — operators running this without policy enforcement should know it.
109	
110	### No kernel-shutdown lifecycle
111	
112	The listener is bound at `init()` and lives until the process exits. SIGTERM closes the TCP socket abruptly — in-flight requests get a TCP RST, not a clean 503. The plugin exposes `closeListener()` as a seam for the kernel-shutdown work in follow-up #3 of `2026-04-25-week-7-9-followups.md`; once that lands, the kernel will call it on SIGTERM and drain in-flight requests cleanly. Until then, abrupt shutdown is the failure mode.
113	
114	### No `crypto.timingSafeEqual` on token compare
115	
116	A reviewer might glance at the auth path and notice we don't run `timingSafeEqual` over the bearer token. That's deliberate, not forgotten.
117	
118	Token resolution is `Map.get(token)` in `@ax/session-inmemory` and `SELECT … WHERE token = $1` in `@ax/session-postgres`. Both are functionally constant-time given uniformly-distributed 256-bit base64url tokens — a timing attacker can't enumerate tokens by probing because the entropy is too high to bisect within any realistic number of requests. There's no second secret to compare against at the listener level — the token resolution **is** the comparison. Adding `timingSafeEqual` on top would compare the token to itself, which doesn't help anyone.
119	
120	We'd rather document the reasoning here than have the next reviewer add a comparison that isn't doing what its name suggests.
121	
122	### No rate limits, no IP allow-list
123	
124	The auth boundary is the allow-list. A token is only mintable via `session:create`, which is an in-process call from the orchestrator. No outside party can mint tokens. Rate limiting against valid tokens is a noisy-neighbor knob (one runner could pummel the host with valid requests), but it's not an authn/authz issue, and it'd need cluster-aware metrics we don't have yet.
125	
126	### Long-poll cap lives in the dispatcher, not here
127	
128	`/session.next-message` long-polls. The 30 s cap (I12) is enforced inside the handler in `@ax/ipc-core`; this listener bumps Node's idle timeout to 60 s so the long-poll isn't killed mid-flight. If a future Node default changes either side, the test in `@ax/ipc-server` (and the analogous one we'll add for HTTP) catches it.
129	
130	## Boundary review
131	
132	- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** none — this plugin doesn't register a service hook. It binds a listener at init() and routes inbound requests to existing hooks (`session:resolve-token`, `llm:call`, `tool:list`, `session:claim-work`, plus the dispatcher-resolved `tool:execute:<name>`). The transport is the contribution; the hook surface is unchanged.
133	- **Payload field names that might leak:** none. The wire payloads are owned by `@ax/ipc-protocol`, which both transports (unix and HTTP) share. No `socket_path`, `port`, `bearer`, `Authorization`, or other transport vocabulary appears on hook payloads — those stay at the listener boundary.
134	- **Subscriber risk:** none new. Subscribers fire on the same hooks the unix listener already drives; `chat:end` / `tool:post-call` / `turn:end` payloads are identical regardless of transport.
135	- **Wire surface (IPC):** this plugin IS a wire surface. The schemas live in `@ax/ipc-protocol` (one schema set, two transports), not in this package, so there's no per-listener schema drift.
136	
137	## What we don't know yet
138	
139	- Whether 4 MiB is the right `MAX_FRAME` cap when image-bytes flow through tool calls. We picked it to match the unix listener; a future image-input feature may need a higher cap on a specific endpoint or a streaming alternative. Today, image-bytes-over-IPC isn't a code path.
140	- How `closeListener()` will compose with in-flight request draining when the kernel-shutdown lifecycle lands. Node's `server.close()` stops accepting new connections and waits for existing ones; we may need to also call `server.closeAllConnections()` after a grace period if a runner is holding a long-poll. Decision deferred to follow-up #3.
141	- Whether the boot-time `process.stderr.write` for the bound address is the right shape once the chart's structured logging lands. It's currently one printf-style line for grep-ability in `kubectl logs`; a future structured-log pass may want to reformat.
142	
143	## Security contact
144	
145	If we find a hole, we'd rather hear about it from you than read about it on Hacker News. Please email `vinay@canopyworks.com`.
146

file.content:
The honest list of what this slice doesn't do, and why.

### Plain HTTP within the cluster, no mTLS

We use plain HTTP between runner pods and the host. NetworkPolicy is the perimeter; bearer auth is the wall behind it. mTLS would be stronger, and we'll add it when a real adversary makes the math change.

This is a documented choice, not an oversight. NetworkPolicy + bearer auth is the same posture legacy v1 used in production, and on a properly-policied cluster it confines reach to the runner pods we just spawned. mTLS adds CA bootstrapping, cert rotation, and a chunk of operational surface; we'd rather get the transport landed and harden later than ship something we can't operate.

The threat we're explicit about: if NetworkPolicy is disabled or unsupported (some kind clusters' default CNI doesn't enforce policies), an attacker reachable on the cluster network could attempt requests against the listener. Bearer auth still blocks the call without a stolen token, and tokens don't leak from the host (they're held in the session store and minted per session). But "NetworkPolicy is the perimeter" is a real prerequisite — operators running this without policy enforcement should know it.

### No kernel-shutdown lifecycle

The listener is bound at `init()` and lives until the process exits. SIGTERM closes the TCP socket abruptly — in-flight requests get a TCP RST, not a clean 503. The plugin exposes `closeListener()` as a seam for the kernel-shutdown work in follow-up #3 of `2026-04-25-week-7-9-followups.md`; once that lands, the kernel will call it on SIGTERM and drain in-flight requests cleanly. Until then, abrupt shutdown is the failure mode.

### No `crypto.timingSafeEqual` on token compare

A reviewer might glance at the auth path and notice we don't run `timingSafeEqual` over the bearer token. That's deliberate, not forgotten.

Token resolution is `Map.get(token)` in `@ax/session-inmemory` and `SELECT … WHERE token = $1` in `@ax/session-postgres`. Both are functionally constant-time given uniformly-distributed 256-bit base64url tokens — a timing attacker can't enumerate tokens by probing because the entropy is too high to bisect within any realistic number of requests. There's no second secret to compare against at the listener level — the token resolution **is** the comparison. Adding `timingSafeEqual` on top would compare the token to itself, which doesn't help anyone.

We'd rather document the reasoning here than have the next reviewer add a comparison that isn't doing what its name suggests.

### No rate limits, no IP allow-list

The auth boundary is the allow-list. A token is only mintable via `session:create`, which is an in-process call from the orchestrator. No outside party can mint tokens. Rate limiting against valid tokens is a noisy-neighbor knob (one runner could pummel the host with valid requests), but it's not an authn/authz issue, and it'd need cluster-aware metrics we don't have yet.

### Long-poll cap lives in the dispatcher, not here

`/session.next-message` long-polls. The 30 s cap (I12) is enforced inside the handler in `@ax/ipc-core`; this listener bumps Node's idle timeout to 60 s so the long-poll isn't killed mid-flight. If a future Node default changes either side, the test in `@ax/ipc-server` (and the analogous one we'll add for HTTP) catches it.

## Boundary review

- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** none — this plugin doesn't register a service hook. It binds a listener at init() and routes inbound requests to existing hooks (`session:resolve-token`, `llm:call`, `tool:list`, `session:claim-work`, plus the dispatcher-resolved `tool:execute:<name>`). The transport is the contribution; the hook surface is unchanged.
- **Payload field names that might leak:** none. The wire payloads are owned by `@ax/ipc-protocol`, which both transports (unix and HTTP) share. No `socket_path`, `port`, `bearer`, `Authorization`, or other transport vocabulary appears on hook payloads — those stay at the listener boundary.
- **Subscriber risk:** none new. Subscribers fire on the same hooks the unix listener already drives; `chat:end` / `tool:post-call` / `turn:end` payloads are identical regardless of transport.
- **Wire surface (IPC):** this plugin IS a wire surface. The schemas live in `@ax/ipc-protocol` (one schema set, two transports), not in this package, so there's no per-listener schema drift.

## What we don't know yet

- Whether 4 MiB is the right `MAX_FRAME` cap when image-bytes flow through tool calls. We picked it to match the unix listener; a future image-input feature may need a higher cap on a specific endpoint or a streaming alternative. Today, image-bytes-over-IPC isn't a code path.
- How `closeListener()` will compose with in-flight request draining when the kernel-shutdown lifecycle lands. Node's `server.close()` stops accepting new connections and waits for existing ones; we may need to also call `server.closeAllConnections()` after a grace period if a runner is holding a long-poll. Decision deferred to follow-up #3.
- Whether the boot-time `process.stderr.write` for the bound address is the right shape once the chart's structured logging lands. It's currently one printf-style line for grep-ability in `kubectl logs`; a future structured-log pass may want to reformat.

## Security contact

If we find a hole, we'd rather hear about it from you than read about it on Hacker News. Please email `vinay@canopyworks.com`.

content.content:
175	- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** `@ax/workspace-postgres-http` would be the same wire surface backed by Postgres-stored snapshots instead of git. The four `workspace:*` hooks don't change shape; the implementation behind them does. The wire schema in `@ax/workspace-protocol` is deliberately git-vocabulary-free — `before` / `after` / `parent` are opaque version strings, not SHAs.
176	- **Payload field names that might leak:** none. The wire schema uses `before`, `after`, `parent`, `version`, `path`, `kind`, `contentBase64`, `contentBeforeBase64`, `contentAfterBase64`, `reason`, `author`. No `commit`, `sha`, `oid`, `tree`, `ref`, or `gitdir`. The `actualParent` / `expectedParent` fields on the parent-mismatch error envelope use the abstract "parent" vocabulary, not "commit" or "sha".
177	- **Subscriber risk:** subscribers must continue to treat `WorkspaceVersion` as opaque (per the workspace-git-core SECURITY note). The HTTP transport doesn't change that contract.
178	- **Wire surface (IPC):** this plugin IS a wire surface. The schemas live in `@ax/workspace-protocol`, not in this package, so there's no per-listener schema drift between the two transports we already share.
179	
180	## What we don't know yet
181	
182	- Whether dual-token acceptance is worth the complexity. The cleaner answer is "rotate during a maintenance window, accept the brief outage." The bigger the deployment gets, the less acceptable that becomes.
183	- How `closeListener()` will compose with in-flight `workspace:apply` draining when the kernel-shutdown lifecycle lands. Today the SIGTERM handler in `main.ts` calls `server.close()` and waits for in-flight requests, which Node implements as "stop accepting new connections, wait for current ones to finish." If a future host long-poll holds a connection open past the grace period, we may need `server.closeAllConnections()` after a deadline.
184	- Whether the per-repo mutex inside `@ax/workspace-git-core` will hold up under the load the multi-replica deployment will throw at it. Today's test exercises 4 concurrent applies; production may see hundreds.
185	
186	## Security contact
187	
188	If we find a hole, we'd rather hear about it from you than read about it on Hacker News. Please email `vinay@canopyworks.com`.
189

file.content:
- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** `@ax/workspace-postgres-http` would be the same wire surface backed by Postgres-stored snapshots instead of git. The four `workspace:*` hooks don't change shape; the implementation behind them does. The wire schema in `@ax/workspace-protocol` is deliberately git-vocabulary-free — `before` / `after` / `parent` are opaque version strings, not SHAs.
- **Payload field names that might leak:** none. The wire schema uses `before`, `after`, `parent`, `version`, `path`, `kind`, `contentBase64`, `contentBeforeBase64`, `contentAfterBase64`, `reason`, `author`. No `commit`, `sha`, `oid`, `tree`, `ref`, or `gitdir`. The `actualParent` / `expectedParent` fields on the parent-mismatch error envelope use the abstract "parent" vocabulary, not "commit" or "sha".
- **Subscriber risk:** subscribers must continue to treat `WorkspaceVersion` as opaque (per the workspace-git-core SECURITY note). The HTTP transport doesn't change that contract.
- **Wire surface (IPC):** this plugin IS a wire surface. The schemas live in `@ax/workspace-protocol`, not in this package, so there's no per-listener schema drift between the two transports we already share.

## What we don't know yet

- Whether dual-token acceptance is worth the complexity. The cleaner answer is "rotate during a maintenance window, accept the brief outage." The bigger the deployment gets, the less acceptable that becomes.
- How `closeListener()` will compose with in-flight `workspace:apply` draining when the kernel-shutdown lifecycle lands. Today the SIGTERM handler in `main.ts` calls `server.close()` and waits for in-flight requests, which Node implements as "stop accepting new connections, wait for current ones to finish." If a future host long-poll holds a connection open past the grace period, we may need `server.closeAllConnections()` after a deadline.
- Whether the per-repo mutex inside `@ax/workspace-git-core` will hold up under the load the multi-replica deployment will throw at it. Today's test exercises 4 concurrent applies; production may see hundreds.

## Security contact

If we find a hole, we'd rather hear about it from you than read about it on Hacker News. Please email `vinay@canopyworks.com`.

content.text: These SECURITY.md docs reference this slice in future tense and need updating. Quick targeted edits.

input.old_string:
### No kernel-shutdown lifecycle

The listener is bound at `init()` and lives until the process exits. SIGTERM closes the TCP socket abruptly — in-flight requests get a TCP RST, not a clean 503. The plugin exposes `closeListener()` as a seam for the kernel-shutdown work in follow-up #3 of `2026-04-25-week-7-9-followups.md`; once that lands, the kernel will call it on SIGTERM and drain in-flight requests cleanly. Until then, abrupt shutdown is the failure mode.
input.new_string:
### In-flight requests not actively drained

The plugin's `Plugin.shutdown` slot calls `server.close()`, which stops accepting new connections and waits for in-flight requests to finish on their own. We do **not** call `server.closeAllConnections()` after a grace period, so a runner holding a long-poll past the per-plugin shutdown timeout (10 s) gets a TCP RST when the timeout fires.

For typical traffic this is fine — every endpoint except `/session.next-message` returns within ms, and the long-poll cap is 30 s (well under most operators' SIGTERM grace). For the long-poll case it's a tradeoff: a clean shutdown that waits up to 30 s vs. an abrupt one. We picked "abrupt past the timeout" so a misbehaving long-poll can't hold the process hostage. If a future host endpoint legitimately needs a longer grace, `server.closeAllConnections()` after a deadline is the next move.

content.subject: Task 1: Update Plugin.shutdown doc-comment
content.description: Replace doc-comment on Plugin.shutdown? slot in packages/core/src/plugin.ts:22-36 to reflect kernel-driven lifecycle. No behavioral change. Run pnpm test --filter @ax/core to confirm green. Commit as docs(core).

content.subject: Task 2: Add KernelHandle + bootstrap shutdown
content.description: Central kernel-side change. Add KernelHandle type, change bootstrap return type to Promise<KernelHandle>, add shutdownTimeoutMs/onShutdownError options, implement reverse-topological shutdown loop, add init-failure rollback, idempotent handle. TDD: write 7 failing tests first in bootstrap.test.ts. Commit as feat(core).

content.subject: Task 3: database-postgres shutdown
content.description: Add async shutdown() to createDatabasePostgresPlugin that calls kysely.destroy(). Drop TODO(kernel-shutdown) at packages/database-postgres/src/plugin.ts:60. Commit.

content.subject: Task 4: storage-sqlite shutdown
content.description: Add async shutdown() to createStorageSqlitePlugin that calls db.destroy(). Drop TODO(kernel-shutdown) at packages/storage-sqlite/src/plugin.ts:22. Commit.

content.subject: Task 5: Drop EventbusPostgresPlugin interface
content.description: Delete EventbusPostgresPlugin interface, return plain Plugin from factory. Drop re-export from index.ts. Update tests to drive shutdown via createTestHarness().close() instead of casting to escape-hatch interface. Commit.

content.subject: Task 6: Drop SessionPostgresPlugin interface
content.description: Delete SessionPostgresPlugin interface, return plain Plugin from factory. Drop re-export. Update doc-comment on hand-rolled init-failure rollback (try/catch stays — kernel does NOT call shutdown on the failing plugin). Update tests to use harness.close(). Commit.

content.subject: Task 7: Verify sandbox-k8s cleanup needs
content.description: Verify whether @kubernetes/client-node@1.x retains a per-client http.Agent that needs explicit destroy(). Expected: no — uses node-fetch with global agent. Document finding in plugin.ts. If a per-client agent IS retained, add shutdown(). Commit.

input.new_string: - Whether the git-server pod's SIGTERM grace period is long enough for in-flight `workspace:apply` draining. The host plugin (this package's `createWorkspaceGitHttpPlugin`) is just an HTTP client and has nothing long-lived to clean up — verified during the kernel-shutdown slice. The git-server pod runs as its own process with its own SIGTERM handler in `src/server/main.ts` that calls `server.close()` and waits for in-flight requests. If a future apply takes longer than the kubelet grace period (default 30 s), we may need `server.closeAllConnections()` after a deadline.

1	import { z } from 'zod';
2	
3	export type WorkspaceActionName =
4	  | 'workspace.apply'
5	  | 'workspace.read'
6	  | 'workspace.list'
7	  | 'workspace.diff';
8	
9	// Wire-side change shape: bytes go as base64 strings.
10	// `.strict()` lives on each branch (discriminatedUnion has no top-level
11	// strict; the per-branch strictness still rejects extras at parse time).
12	const WireFileChangeSchema = z.discriminatedUnion('kind', [
13	  z.object({
14	    path: z.string(),
15	    kind: z.literal('put'),
16	    contentBase64: z.string(),
17	  }).strict(),
18	  z.object({
19	    path: z.string(),
20	    kind: z.literal('delete'),
21	  }).strict(),
22	]);
23	
24	export const WorkspaceApplyRequestSchema = z.object({
25	  changes: z.array(WireFileChangeSchema),
26	  parent: z.string().nullable(),
27	  reason: z.string().optional(),
28	}).strict();
29	
30	const WireWorkspaceChangeSchema = z.discriminatedUnion('kind', [
31	  z.object({
32	    path: z.string(),
33	    kind: z.literal('added'),
34	    contentAfterBase64: z.string(),
35	  }).strict(),
36	  z.object({
37	    path: z.string(),
38	    kind: z.literal('modified'),
39	    contentBeforeBase64: z.string(),
40	    contentAfterBase64: z.string(),
41	  }).strict(),
42	  z.object({
43	    path: z.string(),
44	    kind: z.literal('deleted'),
45	    contentBeforeBase64: z.string(),
46	  }).strict(),
47	]);
48	
49	const WireDeltaSchema = z.object({
50	  before: z.string().nullable(),
51	  after: z.string(),
52	  changes: z.array(WireWorkspaceChangeSchema),
53	  reason: z.string().optional(),
54	  author: z.object({
55	    agentId: z.string(),
56	    userId: z.string(),
57	    sessionId: z.string(),
58	  }).optional(),
59	}).strict();
60	
61	export const WorkspaceApplyResponseSchema = z.object({
62	  version: z.string(),
63	  delta: WireDeltaSchema,
64	}).strict();
65	
66	export const WorkspaceReadRequestSchema = z.object({
67	  path: z.string(),
68	  version: z.string().optional(),
69	}).strict();
70	
71	export const WorkspaceReadResponseSchema = z.discriminatedUnion('found', [
72	  z.object({ found: z.literal(true), bytesBase64: z.string() }).strict(),
73	  z.object({ found: z.literal(false) }).strict(),
74	]);
75	
76	export const WorkspaceListRequestSchema = z.object({
77	  pathGlob: z.string().optional(),
78	  version: z.string().optional(),
79	}).strict();
80	
81	export const WorkspaceListResponseSchema = z.object({
82	  paths: z.array(z.string()),
83	}).strict();
84	
85	export const WorkspaceDiffRequestSchema = z.object({
86	  from: z.string().nullable(),
87	  to: z.string(),
88	}).strict();
89	
90	export const WorkspaceDiffResponseSchema = z.object({
91	  delta: WireDeltaSchema,
92	}).strict();
93	
94	export const WORKSPACE_ACTION_PATHS: Record<WorkspaceActionName, string> = {
95	  'workspace.apply': '/workspace.apply',
96	  'workspace.read': '/workspace.read',
97	  'workspace.list': '/workspace.list',
98	  'workspace.diff': '/workspace.diff',
99	};
100

content.text:
The "TS errors" weren't actual code bugs — `packages/workspace-protocol/dist/` had been built against a different Zod version that emitted `z.core.$strict` (Zod 4) types, even though the installed runtime is Zod 3.25.76 (which emits the older `"strict"` literal types). Once `z.infer` couldn't resolve those `$strict` references, every consumer downstream got `unknown`.

Fix: delete stale `dist/`, rebuild from scratch. `dist/` is gitignored, so no commit needed for this — it's a state cleanup, not a code change. The branch's 11 commits are unchanged.

**Final state:**
- `pnpm build` — clean across the entire monorepo
- `pnpm --filter @ax/cli test` — 62/62 pass (incl. the e2e suite that runs `pnpm build` internally)
- All non-Docker-gated tests across the monorepo green
- Branch ready for PR

If this stale-`dist/` failure mode keeps biting people, the fix is to add `tsbuildinfo` invalidation to whatever bumps Zod — but that's a separate concern. For this slice: nothing more to do.

Push and open a PR?

input.command:
gh pr create --title "feat(core): kernel shutdown lifecycle (KernelHandle.shutdown)" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

Adds an optional shutdown lifecycle to the kernel. Plugins that hold long-lived resources (file handles, pg pools, LISTEN clients, HTTP listeners) now get a clean teardown path on SIGINT/SIGTERM and on init failure. Closes follow-up #3 from `docs/plans/2026-04-25-week-7-9-followups.md`.

`bootstrap()` now returns `Promise<KernelHandle>`; `KernelHandle.shutdown()`:

- Walks plugins in **reverse topological** order (the reverse of init's order — a consumer of `database:get-instance` closes before the database plugin closes the pool).
- Runs each `Plugin.shutdown?()` under a per-plugin timeout (default 10 s, configurable via `BootstrapOptions.shutdownTimeoutMs`).
- Isolates per-plugin failures via an optional `onShutdownError` sink — a throwing or hanging plugin doesn't block its peers.
- Is **idempotent** — first call kicks off shutdown and caches the promise; subsequent calls return the cached one.

On init failure, the same shutdown loop runs against plugins **0..N-1** (the ones that already initialized) before re-throwing. The failing plugin's `shutdown()` is NOT called — its init didn't complete, so partial state may not be safe to close. The kernel never installs signal handlers; that's the host's job. The CLI (`main`, `credentials`, `mcp`) and the `serve` binary are wired up, with `serve` getting a second-signal force-exit (130/143).

Three escape-hatch interfaces (`EventbusPostgresPlugin`, `SessionPostgresPlugin`, `IpcHttpPlugin`) are deleted — their `shutdown()` / `closeListener()` methods now sit on the canonical `Plugin.shutdown` slot. Tests that cast through them switch to driving teardown via `createTestHarness().close()`.

## Invariants

| | Description | Test |
|---|---|---|
| **I1** | Shutdown order is the reverse of init order | `bootstrap.test.ts` "reverse topological order" |
| **I2** | A throwing or hanging plugin doesn't block its peers | `bootstrap.test.ts` "throwing shutdown" + "hanging shutdown is timed out" |
| **I3** | `handle.shutdown()` is idempotent | `bootstrap.test.ts` "idempotent — second call resolves without re-running" |
| **I4** | Init failure runs reverse-order rollback on plugins 0..N-1 (NOT the failing plugin) | `bootstrap.test.ts` "init failure runs shutdown on plugins 0..N-1" + "rollback isolates failures" |
| **I5** | Kernel never installs signal handlers | `bootstrap.ts` has no `process.on(...)`; hosts wire signals |
| **I6** | Sequential, not concurrent shutdown | `runShutdownLoop` uses `for ... await` |
| **I7** | Shutdown is resource-release only — no final writes, flushes, audits | Reviewer verifies; doc-comment on `Plugin.shutdown` enforces |
| **I8** | No new capability surface (no IPC actions, no wire schemas) | `security-checklist` skill not required |
| **I9** | All `TODO(kernel-shutdown)` comments deleted | `grep` returns 0 |
| **I10** | All escape-hatch interfaces deleted | `grep "EventbusPostgresPlugin\|SessionPostgresPlugin\|IpcHttpPlugin\b"` returns 0 in `src/` |

## Boundary review

- **Alternate impl this hook could have:** none — `Plugin.shutdown` isn't a hook bus surface; it's an optional method on the plugin object. The kernel calls it directly.
- **Payload field names that might leak:** N/A — `shutdown()` takes no arguments and returns `Promise<void>`.
- **Subscriber risk:** N/A — no subscribers.
- **Wire surface (IPC):** N/A — internal lifecycle only.

## Per-plugin sweep

| Plugin | Action | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `@ax/database-postgres` | Added `shutdown()` calling `kysely.destroy()` | PostgresDialect.destroy() closes pg.Pool — no separate `pool.end()` |
| `@ax/storage-sqlite` | Added `shutdown()` calling `db.destroy()` | better-sqlite3 flushes WAL + releases file handle on close |
| `@ax/eventbus-postgres` | Dropped `EventbusPostgresPlugin` interface | `shutdown()` body unchanged — moved to `Plugin.shutdown` slot |
| `@ax/session-postgres` | Dropped `SessionPostgresPlugin` interface | Same. The hand-rolled init-failure cleanup at `plugin.ts:257-279` stays — the kernel rolls back 0..N-1 but NOT the failing plugin, so this plugin owns its own partial-init cleanup |
| `@ax/sandbox-k8s` | No shutdown needed (verified) | `@kubernetes/client-node@1.x` allocates a fresh `http.Agent` per request inside `applySecurityAuthentication`; no per-client agent retained — there's nothing for us to `.destroy()` |
| `@ax/ipc-http` | Dropped `IpcHttpPlugin` interface; folded `closeListener()` into `Plugin.shutdown` | Same pattern as the postgres ones |
| `@ax/eventbus-inprocess`, `@ax/workspace-git`, `@ax/workspace-git-http` (host) | No shutdown needed (verified) | Pure in-memory map / per-call FS ops / per-request HTTP with cleared timers and global agent |

## Host wiring

- **CLI `main()`** (chat, credentials, mcp): wraps work in `try { ... } finally { await handle.shutdown(); }` so storage-sqlite WAL flushes even on the error path.
- **`serve` binary**: SIGINT/SIGTERM closes the HTTP listener, then calls `handle.shutdown()`, then `process.exit(0)`. A second signal mid-shutdown force-exits with 130 (SIGINT) / 143 (SIGTERM).
- SIGINT-during-chat for the one-shot CLI is **out of scope** (would need cancel signals threaded into `chat:run` — separate slice). The try/finally still runs on normal completion + bubbled errors.

## Test plan

- [x] 7 new `@ax/core` tests covering I1–I4 — all pass
- [x] All non-Docker-gated tests across the monorepo (~570 tests across 30+ packages) — green
- [x] `pnpm build` — clean (note: needed a fresh `dist/` rebuild after delete; `workspace-protocol` had stale artifacts compiled against a different Zod minor that used `z.core.$strict` types)
- [x] `grep "TODO(kernel-shutdown)"` → 0 matches
- [x] `grep "EventbusPostgresPlugin|SessionPostgresPlugin|IpcHttpPlugin\b"` → 0 matches in `src/`
- [ ] Manual on a kind cluster: SIGTERM the host pod with the postgres trio loaded, verify with `lsof` / `pg_stat_activity` that no connections linger past process exit. (Not run in this PR's environment — Docker not available locally; expected to run on a cluster machine.)

## Plan

`docs/plans/2026-04-25-kernel-shutdown-lifecycle.md` — written first via `superpowers:writing-plans`, executed via `superpowers:subagent-driven-development` (one fresh subagent per task, in-session reviews).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

attachment.snippet:
1	# Security — `@ax/workspace-git-http`
2	
3	This package puts `@ax/workspace-git-core` behind an HTTP transport so multiple host replicas can share one workspace store. Two surfaces ship together: a **pod-side server** (a Node `http` listener bound to `0.0.0.0:7780` by default, run as the `ax-git-server` binary in the chart's git-server Deployment) and a **host-side plugin** (the `workspace:*` service hook implementations the host pod registers, which forward each call to the server over HTTP).
4	
5	It looks structurally similar to `@ax/ipc-http` — same five-gate listener, same wire-error envelope shape, same `MAX_FRAME` body cap, same defensive `crypto.timingSafeEqual` on the bearer compare. But the auth model is meaningfully different: where `@ax/ipc-http` resolves a per-session token through `session:resolve-token`, this package uses one **static service token** shared between every host pod and the git-server pod. There's no session boundary at the workspace layer (a workspace is owned by the cluster, not a session), so the static token is the gate. We unpack what that means below.
6	
7	This note is the `security-checklist` walk for this slice. Sandbox / capability is the section with the most to say; injection and supply chain are short because we share the validation chokepoint and dependency set with `@ax/workspace-git-core` and `@ax/ipc-core`.
8	
9	## Security review
10	
11	- **Sandbox:** Two new surfaces. The pod-side server opens an inbound TCP listener (default `0.0.0.0:7780`); reach is bounded by the chart's NetworkPolicy (Task 17 — host pods only, runner pods MUST NOT reach), the bearer-token wall behind that perimeter (`crypto.timingSafeEqual` against an env-loaded service token), and the pod's own SecurityContext (non-root UID 1000, all caps dropped, `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` per Task 15). The host-side plugin opens **outbound** HTTP only to one configured `baseUrl` and reads no filesystem, spawns no processes, and reads no env. Neither side imports `child_process`.
12	- **Injection:** Every request body is Zod-validated (`@ax/workspace-protocol`) before any handler touches it. The path-validation chokepoint lives in `@ax/workspace-git-core/src/impl.ts` and runs server-side as the canonical gate. Errors never echo body content; auth errors emit short fixed strings (`missing authorization`, `invalid authorization scheme`, `unknown token`) and the token never appears in any log or error message.
13	- **Supply chain:** No new external runtime dependencies. The transport is `node:http`. The runtime deps are all workspace packages (`@ax/core`, `@ax/ipc-core`, `@ax/workspace-protocol`, `@ax/workspace-git-core`) plus `zod` (already in the workspace). DevDeps match the workspace standard.
14	
15	## Sandbox / capability
16	
17	This is the first plugin we ship that opens an HTTP connection between two non-runner pods (host pod talks to git-server pod). It also widens what "workspace pod compromise" means, so it gets its own walk.
18	
19	### Pod-side server: inbound TCP listener
20	
21	The capability footprint is one inbound TCP port and zero of everything else.
22	
23	#### What capability we grant
24	
25	`server.listen(port, host)` on the git-server process. The default is `0.0.0.0:7780`, configurable via `AX_GIT_SERVER_HOST` / `AX_GIT_SERVER_PORT`. Anyone who can route a TCP connection to that address can attempt requests; inside a Kubernetes pod, "anyone who can route" is whoever the cluster's NetworkPolicy lets through.
26	
27	#### What bounds it
28	
29	Three layers, in order:
30	
31	1. **NetworkPolicy** (Task 17 — `deploy/charts/ax-next/templates/networkpolicies/git-server-network.yaml` once it lands). Ingress is allowed only from host-pod labels in the same namespace; runner pods MUST NOT be permitted, ever — runners don't speak workspace protocol and there's no legitimate reason for one to reach the git-server. This is the perimeter.
32	2. **Bearer auth via `crypto.timingSafeEqual`** against an env-loaded token (`AX_GIT_SERVER_TOKEN`, sourced from the Helm Secret `<release>-git-server-auth`). Missing, malformed, or wrong token returns 401 with a fixed message — the offending value is never echoed (invariant I9, carried over from `@ax/ipc-http`). This is the wall behind the perimeter.
33	3. **Pod SecurityContext** (Task 15's chart). Non-root UID 1000, all caps dropped, `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true`, the PVC is the only writable mount and it's the bare-repo's own gitdir. So even if an attacker pops the process, they're a non-root user with no shell, no writable rootfs, and one PVC-bound mount under their feet.
34	
35	#### Blast radius if this pod is compromised
36	
37	Worth calling out explicitly: if the git-server pod is compromised, the attacker can read **every workspace's content**. Every session, every user, all the bytes ever committed. That's a meaningful escalation from `@ax/workspace-git`'s blast radius (one host pod's PVC, one repo). We trade a smaller-blast-radius single-pod design for a larger-blast-radius shared-store design because multi-replica scaling needs the canonical store somewhere — and "somewhere" with a bare repo behind it is here.
38	
39	The mitigation is depth: NetworkPolicy keeps the listener unreachable from non-host pods, the bearer token keeps it unreachable without credentials, the SecurityContext keeps a compromised process from doing much locally, and the chart's PVC is sized and labeled like the production-grade-storage thing it is. The paranoia adds up.
40	
41	#### Cross-session escalation: not applicable here
42	
43	A subtle but important difference from `@ax/ipc-http`. The IPC HTTP listener uses `session:resolve-token` because a token belongs to exactly one session and the resolved `sessionId` becomes the session this request operates as.
44	
45	There's no session boundary at the workspace layer. A workspace is owned by the cluster, not by a session — multiple sessions share a workspace store, and the host process decides which session is asking. Static service token gates everything. **If the token leaks, every workspace leaks.** That's a trade we're making explicitly: one secret to rotate, one wall to defend, no per-session token plumbing across the workspace API.
46	
47	(If you skim the auth code looking for the cross-session check that lives in the unix listener — there isn't one. The token resolution would normally be it, but there's no second value to compare against here.)
48	
49	#### Process spawn — none
50	
51	The server process imports `node:http`, `node:crypto` (for `timingSafeEqual`), `@ax/core`, `@ax/ipc-core` (for `readJsonBody` / response writers), `@ax/workspace-protocol`, and `@ax/workspace-git-core`. No `child_process`, no `execa`, no shell. `isomorphic-git` (transitively, via `@ax/workspace-git-core`) is pure JavaScript by design.
52	
53	#### Path validation chokepoint
54	
55	Lives in `@ax/workspace-git-core/src/impl.ts` (function `validatePath`, lines 84-136). Runs server-side because the host plugin doesn't import core — only the wire shape crosses the boundary, and the wire shape never carries unvalidated bytes (Zod schemas in `@ax/workspace-protocol` reject shape drift before the dispatcher hands the request to handlers).
56	
57	This is defense-in-depth: the server re-validates as the canonical gate even though Zod has already accepted the shape. If a future client (third-party plugin, bug, what have you) sends a path like `../../../etc/passwd`, Zod accepts it as a string, and `validatePath` rejects it because `..` is on the explicit deny list.
58	
59	### Host-side plugin: outbound HTTP, that's it
60	
61	The host-side plugin's capability footprint:
62	
63	- **Outbound HTTP** to one configured `baseUrl` (validated to be `http:`, no `userinfo`, no other schemes — same posture as `parseRunnerEndpoint` in `@ax/ipc-protocol`). The hostname goes to Node's resolver and the port is bound to `[1, 65535]`. There is no DNS-rebinding mitigation; we trust the cluster's internal DNS.
64	- **Filesystem reach:** none. The plugin doesn't `require('fs')` and doesn't pass paths to anything that does.

... [125 lines truncated] ...

1	# Security — `@ax/ipc-http`
2	
3	This is the first plugin in the codebase to open an inbound TCP listener. It registers no service hooks and binds one process-wide HTTP server (default `0.0.0.0:8080`) on the host pod; runner pods reach it via a Kubernetes `Service`. Every request walks the same five-gate pipeline as `@ax/ipc-server`'s unix listener — method, `/healthz`, content-type, bearer auth, body-size — and lands in `@ax/ipc-core`'s dispatcher. We share that dispatcher with the unix listener on purpose: one Zod-validated handler set, two transports. This note is the `security-checklist` walk for the Week 9.5 landing.
4	
5	## Security review
6	
7	- **Sandbox:** New plugin opens a TCP listener on configurable host:port (default `0.0.0.0:8080`). Reach is bounded by Helm NetworkPolicy `host-network.yaml` (only runner-namespace pods + own-namespace pods may ingress). Bearer auth via `session:resolve-token` is the wall behind the perimeter. Cross-session escalation is blocked by token uniqueness (32 bytes of `crypto.randomBytes`, base64url-encoded; resolution returns the bound `sessionId`).
8	- **Injection:** Request bodies are JSON, parsed under `MAX_FRAME` (4 MiB) cap. Each handler's Zod schema rejects shape drift before the body reaches plugin code. Errors never echo body content (`writeJsonError` emits a fixed safe envelope).
9	- **Supply chain:** No new external deps. Node built-in `node:http` is the transport. Workspace deps only: `@ax/core`, `@ax/ipc-core`, `@ax/ipc-protocol`. DevDeps match the workspace standard.
10	
11	## Sandbox / capability
12	
13	The capability footprint is one inbound TCP port and zero of everything else.
14	
15	### What capability we grant
16	
17	`server.listen(port, host)` on the host process. The default is `0.0.0.0:8080`, which means "any client that can route a TCP connection to that address can attempt requests." Inside a Kubernetes pod, "anyone who can route to it" is whoever the cluster's NetworkPolicy lets through.
18	
19	### What bounds it
20	
21	Two layers, in order:
22	
23	1. **NetworkPolicy `host-network.yaml`** (Helm chart, `deploy/charts/ax-next/templates/networkpolicies/agent-runtime-network.yaml`). Ingress is allowed from runner-namespace pods labeled `ax.io/plane: execution` AND from anyone in the host's own namespace (port-forward, in-cluster ingress). Everything else is denied at the CNI layer. This is the perimeter.
24	2. **Bearer auth via `session:resolve-token`** (`@ax/ipc-core/src/auth.ts`). Every request must carry `Authorization: Bearer <token>`. The token is resolved through the session plugin's hook; an unresolved token returns 401 with a fixed `unknown token` message — the offending value is never echoed (I9). This is the wall behind the perimeter.
25	
26	### Path traversal — none
27	
28	Request paths route through a fixed in-code map in `@ax/ipc-core/src/dispatcher.ts`: `/llm.call`, `/tool.list`, `/tool.pre-call`, `/tool.execute-host`, `/workspace.commit-notify`, `/session.next-message`, plus three event endpoints. Anything else hits the unknown-path branch and returns 404 with `unknown path: <pathname>`. There is no filesystem lookup keyed off the URL.
29	
30	### Process spawn — none
31	
32	This plugin imports `node:http`, `@ax/core`, `@ax/ipc-core`. No `child_process`, no `execa`, no shell. The dispatcher and handlers don't spawn either; they call hooks on the bus, which run in-process.
33	
34	### Env exfiltration — none
35	
36	We don't read `process.env` anywhere in this plugin. `host` and `port` come from caller config, not from env. The boot-time `[ax/ipc-http] listening on http://...` line is intentional observability for the chart's manual acceptance test.
37	
38	### Handle leak — none
39	
40	The per-request `ChatContext` is built from `auth.sessionId` and `auth.workspaceRoot` — both come from `session:resolve-token`'s vetted output. We do not pass raw sockets, file descriptors, or any other capability handles across hooks. The dispatcher hands handlers a `ChatContext` and a `HookBus`; that is all the reach a handler gets.
41	
42	### Cross-session escalation — implicit in token resolution
43	
44	A subtle difference from the unix listener: `@ax/ipc-server` binds one socket per session and adds a listener-level cross-session check (the resolved sessionId must match the listener's owning session, else 403). The HTTP listener is **process-wide** — one socket serves every session. There is no listener-owning session to check against.
45	
46	That's safe because the token resolution **is** the cross-session check. A token belongs to exactly one session; `session:resolve-token` returns the bound `sessionId` and that's the session this request operates as. There is no path by which a token for session A can be authenticated as session B. We documented this carefully in `listener.ts` so a future reviewer doesn't reflexively port the unix listener's 403 gate to a context where it has no second value to compare against.
47	
48	## Prompt injection / untrusted content
49	
50	The model can influence one thing this plugin sees: the bytes inside an HTTP request body sent by a runner pod. Nothing else from the model reaches us directly.
51	
52	### Untrusted strings entering the slice
53	
54	- **HTTP request bodies** from runner pods. Read by `readJsonBody` (`@ax/ipc-core/src/body.ts`) under the `MAX_FRAME` cap (4 MiB, defined once in `@ax/core/src/ipc/framing.ts`). The reader fails fast on `Content-Length > cap` (413 before any body bytes are buffered) and also enforces the cap mid-stream against clients that lie in `Content-Length` or use chunked encoding.
55	- **`Authorization` header** value. Compared case-insensitively to the literal prefix `bearer` followed by a space, and the rest is treated as opaque base64url. The token never appears in any log or error message.
56	
57	### Bad destinations — gated by Zod
58	
59	Each handler runs the action's Zod schema (`@ax/ipc-protocol/src/actions.ts`) before plugin code touches the payload. The schemas don't accept shell strings, filesystem paths, or anything we'd want to interpolate downstream — they accept the action's specific shape and reject everything else with 400 `VALIDATION`.
60	
61	The handlers themselves are shared with `@ax/ipc-server`. The transport changed; the validation didn't.
62	
63	### Errors never echo body content
64	
65	`writeJsonError` (`@ax/ipc-core/src/response.ts`) emits a fixed envelope: `{ error: { code, message } }`, where `message` is whatever the handler chose. Auth errors emit short generic strings (`missing authorization`, `invalid authorization scheme`, `unknown token`) — never the offending token value. Per-handler errors include the action name and a sanitized cause; never a body excerpt. This is invariant I9.
66	
67	### Worst case
68	
69	A malicious runner pod sends:
70	
71	- An oversized body → `TooLargeError` → 413, socket destroyed before memory fills.
72	- A body that `JSON.parse` rejects → `BadJsonError` → 400 with `invalid json: <parser error message>`. The parser's message is bounded — `JSON.parse` errors don't echo body content; they say things like `Unexpected token X in JSON at position N`.
73	- A well-formed JSON body with the wrong shape → Zod rejects → 400 `VALIDATION` with the action name and a sanitized error.
74	- A well-formed JSON body that passes Zod but encodes injection-flavored content (e.g. a tool name like `; rm -rf /`) → handler runs, but no handler interpolates inputs into a shell, an SQL string, or a filesystem path. The payload reaches `chat:end` / `tool:post-call` subscribers as untrusted bytes (per ax-conventions I5), and downstream subscribers are responsible for treating it as untrusted.
75	
76	## Supply chain
77	
78	No new external dependencies. The transport is `node:http`, a Node built-in.
79	
80	### Runtime deps
81	
82	- `@ax/core` — workspace, pinned via `workspace:*` (lockfile pins).
83	- `@ax/ipc-core` — workspace, `workspace:*`. Owns the dispatcher, handlers, body reader, auth middleware, response writers.
84	- `@ax/ipc-protocol` — workspace, `workspace:*`. Owns the Zod schemas and the wire-error envelope type.
85	
86	### DevDeps (workspace standard)
87

**`verifyCalls` failure leaks initialized plugins (rollback gap).**

`verifyCalls(plugins, bus)` runs at line 97, after every plugin in `order` has completed `init()`. If it throws `missing-service`, the function throws without invoking `runShutdownLoop(initialized, ...)`, so already-initialized plugins (DB pools, HTTP listeners, LISTEN clients, etc.) never get their `shutdown()` called. Symmetry with the in-loop rollback at lines 83-94 is broken.

This is reachable in practice for any misconfigured plugin set whose `calls` reference services no peer registers — exactly the case where a misconfigured deploy gets stuck holding an open pg pool until the process exits.


<details>
<summary>🛡️ Proposed fix</summary>

```diff
-  verifyCalls(plugins, bus);
+  try {
+    verifyCalls(plugins, bus);
+  } catch (err) {
+    await runShutdownLoop(initialized, shutdownTimeoutMs, onShutdownError);
+    throw err;
+  }
```

```suggestion
async function runShutdownLoop(
  plugins: Plugin[],
  timeoutMs: number,
  onError: (pluginName: string, err: unknown) => void,
): Promise<void> {
  for (let i = plugins.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
    const p = plugins[i]!;
    if (typeof p.shutdown !== 'function') continue;
    try {
      await withTimeout(
        Promise.resolve(p.shutdown()),
        timeoutMs,
        `plugin '${p.manifest.name}' shutdown exceeded ${timeoutMs}ms`,
      );
    } catch (err) {
      try {
        onError(p.manifest.name, err);
      } catch {
        // Sink itself failed; swallow so peer plugins still shut down.
      }
    }
  }
}
```

</details>

Author: coderabbitai[bot]
File: packages/core/src/bootstrap.ts:114
Body: _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_

**`verifyCalls` failure leaks initialized plugins (rollback gap).**

`verifyCalls(plugins, bus)` runs at line 97, after every plugin in `order` has completed `init()`. If it throws `missing-service`, the function throws without invoking `runShutdownLoop(initialized, ...)`, so already-initialized plugins (DB pools, HTTP listeners, LISTEN clients, etc.) never get their `shutdown()` called. Symmetry with the in-loop rollback at lines 83-94 is broken.

This is reachable in practice for any misconfigured plugin set whose `calls` reference services no peer registers — exactly the case where a misconfigured deploy gets stuck holding an open pg pool until the process exits.


<details>
<summary>🛡️ Proposed fix</summary>

```diff
-  verifyCalls(plugins, bus);
+  try {
+    verifyCalls(plugins, bus);
+  } catch (err) {
+    await runShutdownLoop(initialized, shutdownTimeoutMs, onShutdownError);
+    throw err;
+  }
```
</details>

<!-- suggestion_start -->

<details>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

Author: coderabbitai[bot]
File: packages/core/src/bootstrap.ts:140
Body: _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_

**A throwing `onError` sink aborts the shutdown loop.**

`onError(p.manifest.name, err)` at line 137 is inside the `catch` block but not itself guarded. If a host-supplied sink throws (e.g., a structured logger that asserts on schema, a metric library that throws on bad labels), the for loop exits, the remaining plugins' `shutdown()` is skipped, and `runShutdownLoop` rejects — violating the "per-plugin failures never block peer plugins" invariant documented at lines 117-119 and in `plugin.ts`.

The default stderr sink is unlikely to throw, but the contract should not depend on that.


<details>
<summary>🛡️ Proposed fix</summary>

```diff
     } catch (err) {
-      onError(p.manifest.name, err);
+      try {
+        onError(p.manifest.name, err);
+      } catch {
+        // Sink itself failed; swallow so peer plugins still shut down.
+      }
     }
```
</details>

<!-- suggestion_start -->

Body: _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_

**Minor: doc claim "30 s well under most operators' SIGTERM grace" is borderline.**

The Kubernetes default `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` is 30 s — same as the long-poll cap stated here. So in the default-configured cluster, a long-poll started right before SIGTERM lands would not finish within the kubelet's grace and the pod gets a SIGKILL. Consider reframing as "matches" or "at the boundary of" rather than "well under," or noting that operators must explicitly raise `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` to keep long-polls clean.

This also interacts with the per-plugin 10 s shutdown timeout already noted at line 142 — the more pressing case (10 s shutdown timeout < 30 s long-poll cap) is well-documented; the kubelet-grace boundary is the secondary concern.

In `@kubernetes/client-node`, SecurityAuthentication is applied by calling an authenticator’s applyAuthentication(...) on the client’s request options during request construction; there isn’t a special “per-request behavior” toggle for applySecurityAuthentication vs createAgent in the library API. What’s happening per request (relevant mechanics) 1) KubeConfig applies HTTPS and authentication on each request. - KubeConfig has applyAuthorizationHeader(...) which looks up the matching authenticator for the current user (authenticators.find(... isAuthProvider(user))) and then awaits authenticator.applyAuthentication(user, opts). - If the user has a token, it sets opts.headers.Authorization = Bearer ${user.token}. **Add defensive version constraints and TODO for future minor-version bumps.**

The `@kubernetes/client-node` package is correctly pinned to `1.4.0` in package.json, which mitigates the main risk from the original comment: a silent upgrade introducing Agent caching would break the assumption that no socket cleanup is needed at shutdown.

However, the specific technical claim about per-request Agent allocation (in `applySecurityAuthentication` with no long-lived client-level Agent) is difficult to verify without examining the actual library source, and web research suggests the Agent lifecycle is authentication-provider-specific.

- Document the version constraint in code with a TODO referencing the issue (e.g., a comment in `plugin.ts` stating why `1.4.0` is pinned and when to revisit on minor-version bumps).

handle.shutdown() in afterAll. Reverse-topological order closes the
ipc-http listener, session-postgres LISTEN client, and pg pools in
the right sequence — the same teardown the production SIGTERM path
runs.

caught the gap." 2>&1 | tail -5

content.text: 77/77 (was 75; +2 new tests). Both fixes verified.

input.command:
git add packages/core/src/bootstrap.ts packages/core/src/__tests__/bootstrap.test.ts && git commit -m "fix(core): close rollback gaps in bootstrap shutdown

Two CodeRabbit minors on the original PR, both real:

1. verifyCalls(plugins, bus) ran AFTER every plugin had initialized
   but BEFORE the kernel returned its handle. If it threw
   missing-service, no rollback ran and the initialized plugins
   leaked their resources. Wrap it in try/catch + runShutdownLoop so
   the symmetry with the in-loop init-failure rollback holds.

2. runShutdownLoop's onError(p.manifest.name, err) call wasn't itself
   guarded. A host-supplied sink that throws (structured logger that
   asserts on schema, metric library that throws on bad labels)
   would abort the for-loop and skip the remaining plugins'
   shutdowns — a violation of I2 'per-plugin failures never block
   peer plugins.' Wrap the sink call in try/catch and swallow.

Two new tests in bootstrap.test.ts cover both cases per the bug-fix
policy: verifyCalls failure must roll back, and a throwing sink must
not abort the loop.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>"
